title: Ensuring Server Availability Combining Monitoring And Diagnostics
summary: Introduction You want a practical, repeatable way to "Ensure Server Availability: Combining Monitoring and Diagnostics" using the Quantum Network Monitor Assistant. content: Introduction You want a practical, repeatable way to "Ensure Server Availability: Combining Monitoring and Diagnostics" using the Quantum Network Monitor Assistant. Below is a blog-style walkthrough that shows a multi-step approach: review existing monitoring, inspect logs, run active network/security scans (Nmap), perform local diagnostics, and update monitoring to prevent recurrence. The post is written as a realistic conversation between a user and the assistant and demonstrates the exact function calls you would make with the Quantum Network Monitor Assistant. Try these steps yourself at the Quantum Network Monitor Assistant hub: https://devwww.readyforquantum.com/?assistant=open Step 0 — Quick context and goal User: "My production web server is intermittently unavailable. I want to confirm availability, find the root cause (network, service, or config), and make sure monitoring catches it in the future." Assistant: "Great — we'll follow a 6-step flow: 1) Confirm what agents and hosts are already set up 2) Review recent monitoring logs 3) Run an active Nmap/service scan for service-level issues 4) Do network diagnostics (ping/traceroute) from a nearby agent 5) Update or add monitoring checks and alerting 6) Reset alerts and verify" Step 1 — Check available monitoring agents and pick one Rationale: Choose an agent located close to the server (or a "local" agent if you run one on-prem) so diagnostics reflect the real network path. Assistant → (call get agents) How we'd process the response: The function returns a list of agents and their locations. We scan the returned list for an agent close to your server's network (same region or a local agent string that includes your email). If no local agent exists, we recommend installing an agent or selecting a nearby public agent. Simulated outcome (narrative): The agent list shows "London - UK", "Scanner - EU", and "edge-office-1 (your-email@example.com)". We choose "edge-office-1" because it is local to the customer site. Step 2 — List monitored hosts and find your server(s) Rationale: Confirm whether your server is already monitored and what endpoints (http/https/icmp/nmap) are configured. Assistant → (call get host list) How we'd process the response: Inspect the returned host entries for endpoint, port, agent location, enabled status, and last check time. This tells us whether HTTP/HTTPS/ICMP or Nmap checks exist and whether they are enabled. Simulated outcome (narrative): The host record exists, endpoint http is enabled (port 80), but there is no nmap or icmp check configured. Last successful check was 45 minutes ago; several recent alerts exist. Step 3 — Pull recent monitoring logs for the host Rationale: Check the monitoring dataset for failure patterns (timing, response codes, latency spikes) to determine if outages are transient or sustained. Assistant → (call get host data) How we'd process the response: Look at the recent records for alert flag (failed checks), response time, HTTP status codes, and agent location. If multiple agents report timeouts or errors, it's likely server-side. If only one agent reports issues, suspect a regional network issue. Simulated outcome (narrative): The dataset shows repeated HTTP 502 errors and increased latency right before the outages; ICMP is not monitored so we can't tell if the host responded to ping. Decision point: We need an active network/service scan (Nmap) to discover service-level problems and open ports, and we want ICMP to verify network reachability. Step 4 — Run an Nmap service scan via the Security Expert Rationale: Nmap will reveal which TCP ports and services are reachable and their version banners — useful to detect crashed services or firewall changes. Assistant → (call security expert) How we'd process the response: The Security Expert returns Nmap output summarizing open/closed ports, service names/versions, and optionally script results. We parse for closed services that should be open, unexpected services, and fir…
author: Mahadeva
